planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or bare-root plants in early spring; in zone 8 plant February–March. Viking aronia is self-fertile and produces good crops without a cross-pollinator, though yields improve with another aronia variety nearby. The vigorous shrub tolerates wet, poorly drained soils that would kill most fruiting shrubs — a significant advantage in low-lying garden areas.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained to moist or periodically wet soil; Viking is a Scandinavian-selected cultivar of black chokeberry (Aronia melanocarpa) developed for maximum berry production, selected in Sweden for its very heavy crop load of large, dark purple-black berries on an upright, vigorous shrub. The berries ripen in late August–September and are among the most tannin-rich, astringent fruits in cultivation when eaten raw — the name 'chokeberry' derives from this mouth-puckering astringency. Processed into juice, syrup, wine, jam, or dried, the astringency mellows and the extraordinarily high anthocyanin and polyphenol content — among the highest of any commonly grown fruit — makes aronia products popular as nutritional supplements and functional foods. Viking is one of the highest-yielding aronia cultivars per area, producing 10–20 pounds per mature shrub. The shrub also provides excellent ornamental value with white flower clusters in spring and brilliant orange-to-red fall foliage.
sources: University of Minnesota Extension — Aronia Cultivar Selection
Iowa State University Extension — Viking Aronia Berry
Penn State Extension — Black Chokeberry Production

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root shrubs in early spring; in zone 8 plant February–March. Nero is self-fertile and does not require a cross-pollinator. The shrub is adaptable to a wide range of soils including poorly drained or wet sites where most fruiting shrubs fail. Nero suckers moderately and can form a small colony in suitable conditions — mow around the planting to control spread.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained to moist soil — tolerates periodic wet feet better than most fruiting shrubs; Nero is a Czech-selected black chokeberry cultivar widely used in European commercial aronia production, selected for slightly more compact growth habit than Viking with comparable or superior berry yield relative to plant size. The medium to large, round, blue-black to jet-black berries ripen in late August–September with the characteristic high-tannin, astringent flavor of the species — intensely purple when juiced and among the highest known plant sources of anthocyanins and proanthocyanidins. Nero is the most widely grown aronia variety in European commercial production, where aronia juice and concentrate are major products. The compact, upright-to-rounded shrub fits well into mixed border plantings and provides four-season interest with spring flower clusters, glossy summer foliage, heavy late-summer berry crops, and outstanding fall color.
sources: University of Minnesota Extension — Black Chokeberry Varieties
USDA ARS — Aronia melanocarpa Cultivar Evaluation
Iowa State University Extension — Nero Aronia Berry for Production

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root shrubs in early spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Regent is self-fertile but cross-pollination with another Amelanchier alnifolia cultivar improves fruit set. The compact habit makes Regent outstanding for small gardens, foundation plantings, and edible landscape uses where full-size Amelanchier trees are too large.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Regent is a compact, dwarf saskatoon serviceberry (Amelanchier alnifolia) selected by South Dakota State University, producing a shrub typically 3–5 feet tall rather than the 8–12 foot height of standard saskatoon varieties — making it the most practical Amelanchier for small residential gardens, containers, and formal edible landscape plantings where size is a constraint. The sweet, blueberry-like berries ripen in June–July in clusters of 5–10 and are among the most delicious of any native North American fruit — eaten fresh, made into pies, jams, and wine, or dried like raisins. The flavor is richer and sweeter than blueberry with almond-like notes from the tiny edible seeds. Regent blooms very early in spring (March–April in zone 9), providing one of the first nectar sources for bees and early pollinators. The white flower clusters, attractive summer foliage, and orange-to-red fall color make Regent exceptional as an edible ornamental.
sources: South Dakota State University Extension — Regent Juneberry
University of Saskatchewan — Saskatoon Berry Cultivars
North Dakota State University Extension — Juneberry Production Guide

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root shrubs in early spring; in zone 9 plant January–March. Thiessen is one of the earliest-ripening named saskatoon cultivars and is self-fertile, though cross-pollination improves fruit set. The large, upright shrub-to-small-tree form requires more space than compact types like Regent; allow 8–12 feet between plants for good air circulation and full sunlight to minimize powdery mildew.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile soil; Thiessen is a named saskatoon serviceberry (Amelanchier alnifolia) cultivar selected in Alberta, Canada, widely regarded as one of the best-flavored named saskatoon varieties with very large berries (among the largest of any named cultivar) and a sweet, rich, complex flavor with prominent almond notes from the edible seeds. The very large, round, dark purple-blue berries ripen in early to mid-June — among the earliest of the named cultivars, making Thiessen valuable for extending the fresh fruit season into late spring when few other fruits are available. The upright, vigorous shrub can reach 12–14 feet in height at maturity, forming a small multi-stemmed tree form. Thiessen is popular in commercial saskatoon orchards in the Canadian Prairie provinces, where the saskatoon berry industry produces fruit for fresh market, U-pick, frozen, dried, and processed products. The species is among the most cold-hardy edible fruits in cultivation, surviving temperatures below -40°F.
sources: University of Saskatchewan Horticulture Extension — Thiessen Saskatoon
Alberta Agriculture and Forestry — Saskatoon Berry Cultivar Guide
North Dakota State University Extension — Juneberry Cultivar Selection

planting.note: Plant dormant canes or container plants in spring; in zone 8 plant January–March. Josephine is a fall-bearing (primocane) raspberry that in zone 8 begins ripening its fall primocane crop in late August–September. For a single large fall crop, mow all canes to the ground in late winter; for two crops per season, leave floricanes for an early summer crop followed by the fall primocane harvest.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Josephine is a Cornell University fall-bearing (primocane) raspberry selected for exceptional fruit quality — widely considered to have the finest flavor of any fall-bearing raspberry released by the Cornell small fruit breeding program, with very large, conical, bright red berries and a sweet, complex, intensely raspberry-flavored aroma and taste that rivals the best summer-bearing varieties. The primocane-fruiting habit provides a late-season crop in August–October from first-year canes, avoiding the May–June crowded harvest season when many other soft fruits are also ripe. The berries are soft and best for fresh eating or immediate processing — Josephine is not a commercial shipping variety, but for home garden fresh eating it represents the highest quality among fall-bearing raspberries. The vigorous, productive canes grow 4–6 feet tall and the plants are reliably productive once established, returning annually from the root system.
sources: Cornell University Small Fruit Program — Josephine Raspberry Release
Purdue University Extension — Fall-Bearing Raspberry Varieties
Oregon State University Extension — Josephine Raspberry Performance
